questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the time needed for a $50 \text{ Hz}$ alternating current to change its value from zero to its rms value.
questions.solution:
The equation of an alternating current is $I = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$.

The rms value of the current is $I_{rms} = \dfrac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}}$.

Substituting $I = I_{rms}$ in the equation, we get:

$\dfrac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}} = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$

$\sin(\omega t) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

$\omega t = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

Using $\omega = 2\pi f$, we have:

$2\pi f t = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

$t = \dfrac{1}{8f}$

Given $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$, substituting the value of $f$:

$t = \dfrac{1}{8 \times 50} = \dfrac{1}{400} = 0.0025 \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A household supply of electricity operates at $220 \text{ V}$ (rms value) and $50 \text{ Hz}$. Determine the peak voltage and the minimum possible time in which the voltage can change from its rms value to zero.
questions.solution:
Given $V_{rms} = 220 \text{ V}$ and $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$.

The peak voltage $V_0$ is given by:

$V_0 = \sqrt{2} V_{rms} = \sqrt{2} \times 220 \approx 311 \text{ V}$

The instantaneous voltage is $V = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$.

The minimum time required for the voltage to change from its rms value to zero is the same as the time taken to change from zero to its rms value.

When the voltage is zero, $\omega t_1 = 0$.

When the voltage reaches its rms value, $V = \dfrac{V_0}{\sqrt{2}}$.

$\dfrac{V_0}{\sqrt{2}} = V_0 \sin(\omega t_2) \Rightarrow \sin(\omega t_2) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \omega t_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

The phase difference is $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$.

The time taken $\Delta t$ is:

$\Delta t = \dfrac{\Delta \phi}{\omega} = \dfrac{\pi/4}{2\pi f} = \dfrac{1}{8f}$

Substituting $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$:

$\Delta t = \dfrac{1}{8 \times 50} = \dfrac{1}{400} \text{ s} = 2.5 \text{ ms}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bulb with a rating of $60 \text{ W}$ at $220 \text{ V}$ is connected to a household alternating voltage supply of $220 \text{ V}$. Determine the maximum instantaneous current passing through the filament.
questions.solution:
The power rating of the bulb is $P = 60 \text{ W}$ and the rms voltage is $V_{rms} = 220 \text{ V}$.

The rms current flowing through the bulb is given by:

$I_{rms} = \dfrac{P}{V_{rms}} = \dfrac{60}{220} = \dfrac{3}{11} \text{ A}$

The maximum instantaneous current (peak current) for an alternating current is:

$I_{0} = \sqrt{2} I_{rms}$

$I_{0} = \sqrt{2} \times \dfrac{3}{11} \approx 1.414 \times 0.2727 \approx 0.385 \text{ A}$

Rounding off to two decimal places, we get $0.39 \text{ A}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric bulb is designed to operate at $12 \text{ volts}$ DC. If it is connected to an AC source and produces normal brightness, what is the peak voltage of the source?
questions.solution:
For the bulb to produce normal brightness, the RMS voltage of the AC source must be equal to the DC voltage rating of the bulb.

$V_{rms} = 12 \text{ V}$

The peak voltage $V_{0}$ is related to the RMS voltage by the relation:

$V_{0} = \sqrt{2} V_{rms}$

$V_{0} = 1.414 \times 12$

$V_{0} \approx 17 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel-plate air-capacitor has an area of $20 \text{ cm}^2$ and a plate separation of $0.10 \text{ mm}$. The dielectric strength of air is $3.0 \times 10^6 \text{ V/m}$. Determine the maximum rms voltage of an AC source that can be safely connected to this capacitor.
questions.solution:
The maximum electric field (dielectric strength) is given by $E_{\text{max}} = 3.0 \times 10^6 \text{ V/m}$.

The maximum peak voltage $V_{\text{max}}$ that can be applied across the plates without breakdown is

$V_{\text{max}} = E_{\text{max}} \times d$

Substituting the given value $d = 0.10 \text{ mm} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}$

$V_{\text{max}} = (3.0 \times 10^6) \times 10^{-4} = 300 \text{ V}$

For an AC source, the peak voltage is related to the rms voltage by $V_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{2} V_{\text{rms}}$.

$V_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{max}}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{300}{\sqrt{2}} = 150\sqrt{2} \text{ V}$

$V_{\text{rms}} \approx 150 \times 1.414 = 212.1 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When connected to an AC source, a resistive coil consumes a peak power of $80 \text{ W}$. Determine the energy consumed by the coil over a duration of $100 \text{ seconds}$, given that this time is significantly greater than the time period of the source.
questions.solution:
For a resistive coil connected to an AC source, the instantaneous power is given by $P(t) = P_0 \sin^2(\omega t)$, where $P_0$ is the peak power.

Given the peak power $P_0 = 80 \text{ W}$.

The average power consumed over a duration significantly greater than the time period is:

$P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{P_0}{2} = \dfrac{80}{2} = 40 \text{ W}$

The energy consumed by the coil over a duration of $t = 100 \text{ s}$ is:

$E = P_{\text{avg}} \times t$

$E = 40 \times 100 = 4000 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a discharging $LR$ circuit, the current is described by $i = i_0 e^{-t/\tau}$, where $\tau$ represents the circuit's time constant. Determine the rms current over the interval from $t = 0$ to $t = \tau$.
questions.solution:
The root mean square (rms) current over the time interval from $t = 0$ to $t = \tau$ is given by:

$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\tau} \int_{0}^{\tau} i^2 dt}$

Given $i = i_0 e^{-t/\tau}$, we square the current:

$i^2 = i_0^2 e^{-2t/\tau}$

Now, integrate $i^2$ from $t = 0$ to $t = \tau$:

$\int_{0}^{\tau} i^2 dt = \int_{0}^{\tau} i_0^2 e^{-2t/\tau} dt$

$\int_{0}^{\tau} i^2 dt = i_0^2 \left[ \dfrac{e^{-2t/\tau}}{-2/\tau} \right]_{0}^{\tau}$

$\int_{0}^{\tau} i^2 dt = -\dfrac{i_0^2 \tau}{2} (e^{-2} - e^{0}) = \dfrac{i_0^2 \tau}{2} (1 - e^{-2})$

Substitute this back into the rms formula:

$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\tau} \times \dfrac{i_0^2 \tau}{2} (1 - e^{-2})}$

$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{i_0^2}{2} \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{e^2} \right)}$

$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{i_0^2}{2} \left( \dfrac{e^2 - 1}{e^2} \right)}$

$I_{rms} = \dfrac{i_0}{e} \sqrt{\dfrac{e^2 - 1}{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An oscillator providing an output voltage $\mathcal{E} = 10 \text{ V} \sin \omega t$ is connected to a capacitor of capacitance $10 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$. Determine the peak currents in the circuit for angular frequencies $\omega = 10 \text{ s}^{-1}$, $100 \text{ s}^{-1}$, $500 \text{ s}^{-1}$, and $1000 \text{ s}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The peak current in a purely capacitive circuit is given by $I_0 = \dfrac{V_0}{X_C}$, where $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ is the capacitive reactance.

Substituting $X_C$, we get $I_0 = V_0 \omega C$.

Given $V_0 = 10 \text{ V}$ and $C = 10 \text{ }\mu\text{F} = 10^{-5} \text{ F}$, the peak current is $I_0 = 10 \times \omega \times 10^{-5} = 10^{-4} \omega$.

For $\omega = 10 \text{ s}^{-1}$, $I_0 = 10^{-4} \times 10 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}$.

For $\omega = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$, $I_0 = 10^{-4} \times 100 = 0.01 \text{ A}$.

For $\omega = 500 \text{ s}^{-1}$, $I_0 = 10^{-4} \times 500 = 0.05 \text{ A}$.

For $\omega = 1000 \text{ s}^{-1}$, $I_0 = 10^{-4} \times 1000 = 0.1 \text{ A}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil with an inductance of $0.4 \text{ henry}$ and a resistance of $10 \text{ }\Omega$ is connected to an AC source of $6.5 \text{ V}$, $\dfrac{30}{\pi} \text{ Hz}$. Determine the average power consumed in the circuit.
questions.solution:
Given $L = 0.4 \text{ H}$, $R = 10 \text{ }\Omega$, $V_{rms} = 6.5 \text{ V}$, $f = \dfrac{30}{\pi} \text{ Hz}$.

The angular frequency is $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times \dfrac{30}{\pi} = 60 \text{ rad/s}$.

The inductive reactance is $X_L = \omega L = 60 \times 0.4 = 24 \text{ }\Omega$.

The impedance of the circuit is $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} = \sqrt{10^2 + 24^2} = \sqrt{100 + 576} = \sqrt{676} = 26 \text{ }\Omega$.

The rms current in the circuit is $I_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{Z} = \dfrac{6.5}{26} = \dfrac{1}{4} \text{ A}$.

The average power consumed is $P_{avg} = I_{rms}^2 R = \left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)^2 \times 10 = \dfrac{10}{16} = \dfrac{5}{8} \text{ W}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil with an inductance of $5.0 \text{ mH}$ and negligible resistance is connected to an oscillator that provides an output voltage $\mathcal{E} = 10 \text{ V} \sin \omega t$. Determine the peak currents in the circuit for $\omega = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$, $500 \text{ s}^{-1}$, and $1000 \text{ s}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The peak current in an inductive circuit is given by $I_0 = \dfrac{V_0}{X_L}$, where $X_L = \omega L$ is the inductive reactance.

Given $V_0 = 10 \text{ V}$ and $L = 5.0 \text{ mH} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H}$.

For $\omega = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$:
$X_L = 100 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.5 \text{ } \Omega$
$I_0 = \dfrac{10}{0.5} = 20 \text{ A}$

For $\omega = 500 \text{ s}^{-1}$:
$X_L = 500 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5 \text{ } \Omega$
$I_0 = \dfrac{10}{2.5} = 4.0 \text{ A}$

For $\omega = 1000 \text{ s}^{-1}$:
$X_L = 1000 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} = 5.0 \text{ } \Omega$
$I_0 = \dfrac{10}{5.0} = 2.0 \text{ A}$

The peak currents are $20 \text{ A}$, $4.0 \text{ A}$, and $2.0 \text{ A}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An AC source given by $\mathcal{E} = (12 \text{ V}) \sin\!\left((250\pi \text{ s}^{-1}) t\right)$ is connected across a resistor of resistance $100 \text{ }\Omega$. Determine the energy dissipated as heat from $t = 0$ to $t = 1.0 \text{ ms}$.
questions.solution:
The instantaneous power dissipated in the resistor is given by $P = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}^2}{R}$.

The energy dissipated as heat from $t = 0$ to $t = 1.0 \text{ ms}$ is:

$H = \int_{0}^{t} P dt = \int_{0}^{10^{-3}} \dfrac{(12 \sin(250\pi t))^2}{100} dt$

$H = 1.44 \int_{0}^{10^{-3}} \sin^2(250\pi t) dt$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2 \theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$H = 1.44 \int_{0}^{10^{-3}} \dfrac{1 - \cos(500\pi t)}{2} dt$

$H = 0.72 \left[ t - \dfrac{\sin(500\pi t)}{500\pi} \right]_{0}^{10^{-3}}$

Substituting the limits:

$H = 0.72 \left( 10^{-3} - \dfrac{\sin(500\pi \times 10^{-3})}{500\pi} \right)$

$H = 0.72 \left( 10^{-3} - \dfrac{\sin(\pi/2)}{500\pi} \right)$

$H = 0.72 \times 10^{-3} \left( 1 - \dfrac{2}{\pi} \right)$

Taking $\pi \approx 3.1416$:

$H \approx 0.72 \times 10^{-3} \left( 1 - 0.6366 \right)$

$H \approx 0.72 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3634$

$H \approx 2.61 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric bulb is rated to consume $55 \text{ W}$ when operated at $110 \text{ V}$. It is connected in series with a choke coil across a $220 \text{ V}$, $50 \text{ Hz}$ supply. Determine the inductance of the coil required for the bulb to receive its correct voltage.
questions.solution:
The rated current of the electric bulb is $I = \dfrac{P}{V_R} = \dfrac{55}{110} = 0.5 \text{ A}$

The resistance of the bulb is $R = \dfrac{V_R}{I} = \dfrac{110}{0.5} = 220 \ \Omega$

When connected in series with a choke coil across a $220 \text{ V}$ supply, the total voltage is $V = 220 \text{ V}$.

In a series LR circuit, the supply voltage is given by $V^2 = V_R^2 + V_L^2$

$(220)^2 = (110)^2 + V_L^2$

$V_L = \sqrt{220^2 - 110^2} = 110\sqrt{3} \text{ V}$

The voltage across the inductor is also given by $V_L = I X_L = I (2\pi f L)$

$110\sqrt{3} = 0.5 \times 2\pi \times 50 \times L$

$110\sqrt{3} = 50\pi L$

$L = \dfrac{110\sqrt{3}}{50\pi} = \dfrac{2.2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} \approx \dfrac{2.2 \times 1.732}{3.14} \approx 1.2 \text{ H}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a series $LCR$ circuit connected to an AC source, the parameters are given as $R = 300 \text{ }\Omega$, $C = 20 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $L = 1.0 \text{ henry}$, $\mathcal{E}_{\text{rms}} = 50 \text{ V}$, and $\nu = 50/\pi \text{ Hz}$. Determine the rms current in the circuit. (Observe that the sum of the rms potential differences across the three elements exceeds the rms voltage of the source.)
questions.solution:
The angular frequency of the AC source is given by:

$\omega = 2\pi\nu = 2\pi \times \dfrac{50}{\pi} = 100 \text{ rad/s}$

The inductive reactance $X_L$ is:

$X_L = \omega L = 100 \times 1.0 = 100 \text{ }\Omega$

The capacitive reactance $X_C$ is:

$X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}} = 500 \text{ }\Omega$

The impedance $Z$ of the series $LCR$ circuit is:

$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_C - X_L)^2}$

$Z = \sqrt{300^2 + (500 - 100)^2} = \sqrt{300^2 + 400^2} = 500 \text{ }\Omega$

The rms current in the circuit is:

$I_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}_{\text{rms}}}{Z} = \dfrac{50}{500} = 0.10 \text{ A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An AC source is connected to a series $RC$ circuit where $R = 300 \text{ }\Omega$, $C = 25 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $\mathcal{E}_0 = 50 \text{ V}$, and $\nu = 50/\pi \text{ Hz}$. Determine the peak current and the average power dissipated in the circuit.
questions.solution:
Angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi\nu = 2\pi \times \dfrac{50}{\pi} = 100 \text{ rad/s}$

Capacitive reactance $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}} = 400 \text{ }\Omega$

Impedance of the circuit $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2} = \sqrt{300^2 + 400^2} = 500 \text{ }\Omega$

Peak current $I_0 = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}_0}{Z} = \dfrac{50}{500} = 0.10 \text{ A}$

Average power dissipated $P_{avg} = I_{rms}^2 R = \left(\dfrac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 R$

$P_{avg} = \dfrac{(0.10)^2}{2} \times 300 = 1.5 \text{ W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a series $LCR$ circuit connected to an AC source, the parameters are $R = 300 \text{ }\Omega$, $C = 20 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $L = 1.0 \text{ henry}$, $\mathcal{E}_{\text{rms}} = 50 \text{ V}$, and $\nu = 50/\pi \text{ Hz}$. Determine the average electric field energy stored in the capacitor and the average magnetic field energy stored in the coil.
questions.solution:
The angular frequency of the AC source is given by:

$\omega = 2\pi\nu = 2\pi \times \dfrac{50}{\pi} = 100 \text{ rad/s}$

The inductive reactance $X_L$ is:

$X_L = \omega L = 100 \times 1.0 = 100 \text{ }\Omega$

The capacitive reactance $X_C$ is:

$X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}} = 500 \text{ }\Omega$

The impedance $Z$ of the series LCR circuit is:

$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_C - X_L)^2} = \sqrt{300^2 + (500 - 100)^2} = \sqrt{300^2 + 400^2} = 500 \text{ }\Omega$

The RMS current in the circuit is:

$I_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}_{\text{rms}}}{Z} = \dfrac{50}{500} = 0.1 \text{ A}$

The RMS voltage across the capacitor is:

$V_{C,\text{rms}} = I_{\text{rms}} X_C = 0.1 \times 500 = 50 \text{ V}$

The average electric field energy stored in the capacitor is:

$U_C = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_{C,\text{rms}}^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \times (50)^2 = 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 25 \text{ mJ}$

The average magnetic field energy stored in the inductor is:

$U_L = \dfrac{1}{2} L I_{\text{rms}}^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.0 \times (0.1)^2 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 5 \text{ mJ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a series $LCR$ circuit connected to an AC source, the parameters are given as $R = 300 \text{ }\Omega$, $C = 20 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $L = 1.0 \text{ henry}$, $\mathcal{E}_{\text{rms}} = 50 \text{ V}$, and $\nu = 50/\pi \text{ Hz}$. Determine the rms potential differences across the capacitor, the resistor, and the inductor, respectively. (Observe that the sum of the rms potential differences across the three elements exceeds the rms voltage of the source.)
questions.solution:
The angular frequency of the AC source is given by:

$\omega = 2\pi \nu = 2\pi \left(\dfrac{50}{\pi}\right) = 100 \text{ rad/s}$

The capacitive reactance $X_C$ is:

$X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}} = 500 \text{ }\Omega$

The inductive reactance $X_L$ is:

$X_L = \omega L = 100 \times 1.0 = 100 \text{ }\Omega$

The impedance $Z$ of the series $LCR$ circuit is:

$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_C - X_L)^2} = \sqrt{300^2 + (500 - 100)^2} = \sqrt{300^2 + 400^2} = 500 \text{ }\Omega$

The rms current $I_{\text{rms}}$ in the circuit is:

$I_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}_{\text{rms}}}{Z} = \dfrac{50}{500} = 0.1 \text{ A}$

The rms potential difference across the capacitor is:

$V_C = I_{\text{rms}} X_C = 0.1 \times 500 = 50 \text{ V}$

The rms potential difference across the resistor is:

$V_R = I_{\text{rms}} R = 0.1 \times 300 = 30 \text{ V}$

The rms potential difference across the inductor is:

$V_L = I_{\text{rms}} X_L = 0.1 \times 100 = 10 \text{ V}$

Thus, the potential differences across the capacitor, resistor, and inductor are $50 \text{ V}$, $30 \text{ V}$, and $10 \text{ V}$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductance of $2.0 	ext{ H}$, a capacitance of $18 	ext{ }\mu\text{F}$ and a resistance of $10 	ext{ k}\Omega$ are connected to an AC source of $20 	ext{ V}$ with an adjustable frequency. Determine the frequency that must be chosen to maximise the current in the circuit.
questions.solution:
The current in an LCR circuit is maximum at the resonant frequency.

The resonant frequency is given by $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$

Substituting the given values $L = 2.0 \text{ H}$ and $C = 18 \mu\text{F} = 18 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$:

$f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{2.0 \times 18 \times 10^{-6}}}$

$f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{36 \times 10^{-6}}}$

$f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}$

$f = \dfrac{1000}{12\pi}$

$f = \dfrac{250}{3\pi} \text{ Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductor coil, a capacitor, and an AC source with an rms voltage of $24 \text{ V}$ are connected in series. A maximum rms current of $6.0 \text{ A}$ is observed when the frequency of the source is varied. Determine the current if this inductor coil is connected to a battery of emf $12 \text{ V}$ and internal resistance $4.0 \text{ }\Omega$.
questions.solution:
In an AC circuit, the maximum rms current occurs at resonance when the inductive reactance equals the capacitive reactance. At this frequency, the impedance of the circuit is purely resistive and is equal to the resistance of the inductor coil, $R$.

The resistance of the coil is given by:

$R = \dfrac{V_{\text{rms}}}{I_{\text{rms}}} = \dfrac{24}{6.0} = 4.0 \text{ }\Omega$

When the inductor coil is connected to a DC battery, it offers zero inductive reactance in the steady state. The total resistance in the circuit is the sum of the coil's resistance and the battery's internal resistance.

$R_{\text{total}} = R + r = 4.0 + 4.0 = 8.0 \text{ }\Omega$

The steady-state current from the battery is:

$I = \dfrac{E}{R_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{12}{8.0} = 1.5 \text{ A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductance of $2.0 	ext{ H}$, a capacitance of $18 	ext{ }\mu\text{F}$ and a resistance of $10 	ext{ k}\Omega$ are connected to an AC source of $20 	ext{ V}$ with an adjustable frequency. Determine the value of the maximum current in the circuit.
questions.solution:
The current in a series LCR circuit is given by:

$I = \dfrac{V}{Z}$

where the impedance $Z$ is:

$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(X_L - X_C\right)^2}$

The current in the circuit is maximum when the impedance is minimum. Since the frequency is adjustable, the minimum impedance occurs at the resonant frequency where the inductive reactance equals the capacitive reactance ($X_L = X_C$).

At resonance, the impedance is:

$Z = R$

Therefore, the maximum current is:

$I_{max} = \dfrac{V}{R}$

Given:
$V = 20 \text{ V}$
$R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^4 \text{ } \Omega$

Substituting the values:

$I_{max} = \dfrac{20}{10^4} \text{ A}$

$I_{max} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 2 \text{ mA}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A typical circuit for a low-pass filter is shown in the figure. An AC input $V_i = 10 \text{ mV}$ is applied at the left end, while the output $V_0$ is received at the right end. Determine the output voltages for frequencies $\nu = 10 \text{ kHz}$, $100 \text{ kHz}$, $1.0 \text{ MHz}$, and $10.0 \text{ MHz}$. Observe that as the frequency increases, the output decreases, which accounts for the name low-pass filter.
questions.solution:
The output voltage $V_0$ across the capacitor in an RC series circuit is given by:

$V_0 = \dfrac{V_i X_C}{Z} = \dfrac{V_i \left(\dfrac{1}{\omega C} ight)}{\sqrt{R^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{\omega C} ight)^2}} = \dfrac{V_i}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$

Given values are:
$V_i = 10 ext{ mV}$
$R = 1.0 ext{ k}\Omega = 10^3 ext{ }\Omega$
$C = 10 ext{ nF} = 10 imes 10^{-9} ext{ F} = 10^{-8} ext{ F}$
The time constant is $RC = 10^3 imes 10^{-8} = 10^{-5} ext{ s}$.
Also, $\omega = 2\pi u$.

For $ u = 10 ext{ kHz} = 10^4 ext{ Hz}$:
$\omega RC = 2\pi imes 10^4 imes 10^{-5} = 0.2\pi \approx 0.628$
$V_0 = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{1 + (0.628)^2}} = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{1 + 0.394}} \approx 8.47 ext{ mV} \approx 8.5 ext{ mV}$

For $ u = 100 ext{ kHz} = 10^5 ext{ Hz}$:
$\omega RC = 2\pi imes 10^5 imes 10^{-5} = 2\pi \approx 6.28$
$V_0 = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{1 + (6.28)^2}} = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{1 + 39.4}} \approx 1.57 ext{ mV} \approx 1.6 ext{ mV}$

For $ u = 1.0 ext{ MHz} = 10^6 ext{ Hz}$:
$\omega RC = 2\pi imes 10^6 imes 10^{-5} = 20\pi \approx 62.8$
$V_0 = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{1 + (62.8)^2}} \approx \dfrac{10}{62.8} \approx 0.159 ext{ mV} \approx 0.16 ext{ mV}$

For $ u = 10.0 ext{ MHz} = 10^7 ext{ Hz}$:
$\omega RC = 2\pi imes 10^7 imes 10^{-5} = 200\pi \approx 628$
$V_0 = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{1 + (628)^2}} \approx \dfrac{10}{628} \approx 0.0159 ext{ mV} = 15.9 ext{ }\mu ext{V} \approx 16 ext{ }\mu ext{V}$

The output voltages are approximately $8.5 ext{ mV}$, $1.6 ext{ mV}$, $0.16 ext{ mV}$, and $16 ext{ }\mu ext{V}$.

Answer: $8.5 ext{ mV}$, $1.6 ext{ mV}$, $0.16 ext{ mV}$, $16 ext{ }\mu ext{V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transformer features $50$ turns in the primary and $100$ in the secondary. When the primary is connected to a $220 \text{ V}$ DC supply, determine the voltage across the secondary.
questions.solution:
A transformer operates on the principle of mutual induction, which requires a changing magnetic flux to induce an electromotive force in the secondary coil.

When a DC supply is connected to the primary coil, a constant current flows through it, producing a steady magnetic field. Thus, the magnetic flux linked with the secondary coil does not change with time.

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced EMF is given by $e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.

Since $\Phi$ is constant for a DC supply, $\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = 0$.

Therefore, the voltage induced across the secondary coil is $0$.
